title: Pryaniki
subtitle: Russian spiced honey biscuits with a sugar glaze
excerpt: Pryaniki are soft Russian honey biscuits, warmly spiced and glazed with a thin white sugar coating that dries to a crackled finish. They are closer in texture to a soft gingerbread than a crisp biscuit. Made across Russia and Eastern Europe, they keep for weeks in a tin.
sourceNotes: Pryaniki have been documented in Russian culinary literature since at least the tenth century, making them one of the oldest recorded Russian baked goods. They are historically associated with Tula, a city south of Moscow that remains famous for its distinctive flat round pryaniki stamped with decorative images. The spice blend varies by region and era. This recipe follows the standard home-baking tradition using mixed spice and honey.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unglazed pryaniki in layers separated by baking paper. Thaw at room temperature and glaze once cool.
recipe.batchNotes: The recipe doubles well. Make extra to gift; they improve with a day or two of maturing in a tin.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Pryaniki keep well for up to 2 weeks in an airtight container. The glaze softens slightly over time, which most people prefer to the crackled fresh version.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; small biscuits take 10 to 12 minutes; watch the edges, not the tops, which stay pale

glossaryTerms.definition: The pryaniki glaze is made by dissolving sugar in just enough hot water to produce a thick, runny icing. Brush it onto the warm (not hot) biscuits and leave to dry. As it cools it sets to a crackled white coating. If the glaze is too thin it will run off; if too thick it will not spread. The right consistency coats a spoon thickly and falls off in a thick ribbon.

content.text: Pryaniki are one of the easiest spiced biscuits to make. The dough comes together quickly and bakes without fuss. The
is applied while the biscuits are warm, which helps it adhere before setting crisp. Use any round or simple shaped cutter.

Warm 150 g honey, 80 g caster sugar, and 60 g butter in a small saucepan over low heat until the butter melts and the sugar dissolves. Remove from the heat and cool to lukewarm. Stir in 2 eggs and 1 tsp vanilla. Add 350 g flour, 1 tsp bicarbonate of soda, 1 tsp mixed spice, 1 tsp ground ginger, and 0.5 tsp cinnamon. Mix to a soft, slightly sticky dough. Wrap and refrigerate for 30 to 60 minutes until firm enough to roll.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Roll the dough on a floured surface to 5 mm thickness. Cut into rounds or simple shapes with a cutter. Place on the prepared trays with space between each. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until the edges are set; the tops will stay pale. Cool on the trays for 5 minutes.

Dissolve 150 g granulated sugar in 60 ml hot water, stirring, to make a thick, pourable glaze. Brush onto the warm biscuits. Leave to dry completely on the rack; the glaze will set to a crackled white coating as it cools, usually within 20 minutes.

items.problem: Glaze does not set and stays sticky
items.solution: The glaze was too dilute, or the biscuits were glazed when too hot and the glaze ran off. Make the glaze thicker by using less water. Apply when the biscuits are warm, not hot.

items.problem: Biscuits spread and lose their shape
items.solution: The dough was too warm when it went into the oven. Chill the cut biscuits on the tray in the fridge for 10 minutes before baking, which helps them hold their shape.
